items.objection: We already have Databricks — why do we need SAS?
items.underlyingConcern: Sunk cost. Don't want to displace existing investment. Data engineering team is happy with Databricks.

response.acknowledge: Databricks is a strong platform — your data engineering team's investment makes sense.
response.reframe: We're not asking you to replace Databricks. The question is: does Databricks give you everything you need for regulated production analytics? Specifically: model governance with approval workflows, BSP-format documentation, real-time fraud scoring, and business-user self-service?
response.evidence: Most of our banking customers run Databricks AND SAS. Databricks handles data engineering (ETL, Delta Lake, feature engineering). SAS handles governed analytics (model lifecycle, fraud scoring, regulatory compliance). It's a complementary architecture.
response.close: Would it help to see how other banks architect Databricks + SAS together? I can show you the integration pattern.

proofPoints: Better Together architecture is common in banking
Databricks handles data engineering; SAS handles governed analytics
BSP examiners need more than MLflow notebooks for model documentation
items.whatNotToSay: Don't say 'Databricks is just for data engineering' — it does ML too. Focus on the regulated governance and industry solutions gap.

items.objection: We already have Azure ML / Microsoft Fabric — it's bundled with our EA
items.underlyingConcern: Path of least resistance. Microsoft EA covers many tools. Adding another vendor requires justification. Microsoft account team may be blocking.

response.acknowledge: Having analytics capabilities bundled in your EA is convenient — I understand why that's attractive.
response.reframe: Two questions: First, is Azure ML giving you BSP-grade model governance with formal approval workflows? Second, does it provide pre-built fraud/AML solutions for Philippine regulations? If not — those gaps are what SAS fills. And SAS runs on Azure.
response.evidence: SAS Viya deploys on AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service). We're not asking you to leave Microsoft — we run ON Microsoft. The value-add: regulated governance, industry solutions, and business-user tools that Azure ML doesn't provide. Your Microsoft investment is preserved.
response.close: Can I show you what SAS adds on top of your existing Azure environment? It's additive, not competitive.

proofPoints: SAS Viya runs natively on Azure AKS
Available on Azure Marketplace for simplified procurement
Microsoft and SAS have strategic partnership
items.whatNotToSay: Don't attack Microsoft directly — many customers are loyal to their Microsoft relationship. Position SAS as a complement within Azure.

items.objection: We already invested in a different fraud solution — we can't switch
items.underlyingConcern: Sunk cost fallacy. Implementation fatigue. Political capital spent getting current solution approved. Switching costs seem high.

response.acknowledge: Replacing a system that's already deployed is painful — I wouldn't recommend it lightly.
response.reframe: Let's separate two questions: Is your current solution meeting regulatory requirements? And is it keeping up with evolving fraud patterns? If either answer is 'no', the cost of NOT switching may exceed the switching cost.
response.evidence: BSP Circular 1213 sets a specific bar: real-time behavioral monitoring, network analytics, model governance. Many existing solutions — particularly rules-based systems — don't meet this bar. The cost of non-compliance (sanctions, restrictions) dwarfs migration costs.
response.close: What if we did a gap analysis — comparing your current solution against BSP 1213 requirements? If gaps exist, we can show a phased migration approach rather than a big-bang switch.

proofPoints: BSP 1213 requires capabilities many legacy systems lack
Phased migration reduces risk and spreads cost
Cost of non-compliance: potential sanctions and operational restrictions
items.whatNotToSay: Don't say their current solution is 'bad' — respect the decision that was made. Focus on whether it meets FUTURE requirements.
